initial_claims.location: splitDAO() and refund() - the historic DAO hack vulnerability
initial_claims.description: In splitDAO(), the external call p.splitData[0].newDAO.createTokenProxy.value(fundsToBeMoved)(msg.sender) is made before balances[msg.sender] is zeroed. An attacker controlling the new DAO can reenter splitDAO() repeatedly, each time passing the balance check since balances[msg.sender] is still non-zero, draining funds proportional to their token balance multiple times. Similarly, refund() calls msg.sender.call.value(weiGiven[msg.sender])() before setting weiGiven[msg.sender] = 0 and balances[msg.sender] = 0.
initial_claims.evidence: splitDAO(): 'if (p.splitData[0].newDAO.createTokenProxy.value(fundsToBeMoved)(msg.sender) == false) throw;' executes before 'totalSupply -= balances[msg.sender]; balances[msg.sender] = 0;'. refund(): 'if (msg.sender.call.value(weiGiven[msg.sender])())' executes before 'balances[msg.sender] = 0; weiGiven[msg.sender] = 0;'. Attacker deploys malicious contract as newDAO whose createTokenProxy fallback reenters splitDAO() repeatedly before balance is cleared.

claim.location: splitDAO() and refund() - the historic DAO hack vulnerability
claim.description: In splitDAO(), the external call p.splitData[0].newDAO.createTokenProxy.value(fundsToBeMoved)(msg.sender) is made before balances[msg.sender] is zeroed. An attacker controlling the new DAO can reenter splitDAO() repeatedly, each time passing the balance check since balances[msg.sender] is still non-zero, draining funds proportional to their token balance multiple times. Similarly, refund() calls msg.sender.call.value(weiGiven[msg.sender])() before setting weiGiven[msg.sender] = 0 and balances[msg.sender] = 0.
claim.evidence: splitDAO(): 'if (p.splitData[0].newDAO.createTokenProxy.value(fundsToBeMoved)(msg.sender) == false) throw;' executes before 'totalSupply -= balances[msg.sender]; balances[msg.sender] = 0;'. refund(): 'if (msg.sender.call.value(weiGiven[msg.sender])())' executes before 'balances[msg.sender] = 0; weiGiven[msg.sender] = 0;'. Attacker deploys malicious contract as newDAO whose createTokenProxy fallback reenters splitDAO() repeatedly before balance is cleared.

verdict.reasoning: The vulnerable pattern is definitively present in the code. In splitDAO(), the external call `p.splitData[0].newDAO.createTokenProxy.value(fundsToBeMoved)(msg.sender)` is made at line before `totalSupply -= balances[msg.sender]; balances[msg.sender] = 0; paidOut[msg.sender] = 0;`. This is a classic CEI (Checks-Effects-Interactions) violation. The attacker controls the newDAO contract (they proposed it as the new curator), so they control the `createTokenProxy` function. When the DAO sends ETH to the malicious newDAO via `.value(fundsToBeMoved)`, the malicious contract's `createTokenProxy` can reenter `splitDAO()` with the same `_proposalID`. On reentry, `balances[msg.sender]` is still non-zero (it hasn't been cleared yet), so `fundsToBeMoved` is recalculated as the same positive value, and the attacker receives another `fundsToBeMoved` ETH. This can repeat until the DAO is drained. The `p.splitData[0].newDAO` check only prevents creating a new DAO on subsequent calls (since it's already set), but does NOT prevent the reentrancy from proceeding through the rest of the function. Similarly, in `refund()`, `msg.sender.call.value(weiGiven[msg.sender])()` is called before `weiGiven[msg.sender] = 0` and `balances[msg.sender] = 0`, enabling the same reentrancy pattern. No reentrancy guard exists anywhere in the contract. The defender correctly concedes no mitigation exists. This is the historic DAO hack vulnerability that led to the Ethereum/Ethereum Classic split in 2016.
verdict.recommendation: Apply the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: zero out all state variables (balances[msg.sender], totalSupply, paidOut[msg.sender], weiGiven[msg.sender]) BEFORE making any external calls. Alternatively, add a reentrancy mutex (bool locked; modifier noReentrancy { if(locked) throw; locked = true; _; locked = false; }) to splitDAO() and refund().

On reentry, `balances[msg.sender]` is still non-zero (it hasn't been cleared yet), so `fundsToBeMoved` is recalculated as the same positive value, and the attacker receives another `fundsToBeMoved` ETH. This can repeat until the DAO is drained. The `p.splitData[0].newDAO` check only prevents creating a new DAO on subsequent calls (since it's already set), but does NOT prevent the reentrancy from proceeding through the rest of the function. Similarly, in `refund()`, `msg.sender.call.value(weiGiven[msg.sender])()` is called before `weiGiven[msg.sender] = 0` and `balances[msg.sender] = 0`, enabling the same reentrancy pattern. No mitigation exists. The code follows the exact vulnerable pattern described: external calls are made before state variables are zeroed, enabling reentrancy.

The vulnerable pattern is definitively present in the code. In splitDAO(), the external call `p.splitData[0].newDAO.createTokenProxy.value(fundsToBeMoved)(msg.sender)` is made at line before `totalSupply -= balances[msg.sender]; balances[msg.sender] = 0; paidOut[msg.sender] = 0;`. This is a classic CEI (Checks-Effects-Interactions) violation. The attacker controls the newDAO contract (they proposed it as the new curator), so they control the `createTokenProxy` function. When the DAO sends ETH to the malicious newDAO via `.value(fundsToBeMoved)`, the malicious contract's `createTokenProxy` can reenter `splitDAO()` with the same `_proposalID`. On reentry, `balances[msg.sender]` is still non-zero (it hasn't been cleared yet), so `fundsToBeMoved` is recalculated as the same positive value, and the attacker receives another `fundsToBeMoved` ETH. This can repeat until the DAO is drained. The `p.splitData[0].newDAO` check only prevents creating a new DAO on subsequent calls (since it's already set), but does NOT prevent the reentrancy from proceeding through the rest of the function. Similarly, in `refund()`, `msg.sender.call.value(weiGiven[msg.sender])()` is called before `weiGiven[msg.sender] = 0` and `balances[msg.sender] = 0`, enabling the same reentrancy pattern. No reentrancy guard exists anywhere in the contract. The defender correctly concedes no mitigation exists. This is the historic DAO hack vulnerability that led to the Ethereum/Ethereum Classic split in 2016.
